title: Genentech lays off 103 alongside gRED rethink, pens $490M breast cancer collab
summary: Genentech (Roche) has disclosed layoffs of 103 positions tied to a restructuring of its Genentech Research and Early Development (gRED) group, following earlier confirmation that VPs Vishva Dixit, Man-Wah Tan, and Todd McDevitt were among those affected. This joins prior rounds in 2024 totaling at least 658 eliminated jobs (489 plus 436). Concurrently, Genentech agreed to a deal granting Astex Pharmaceuticals up to $490 million in milestone payments plus tiered royalties for an exclusive license to Astex's fragment-based drug discovery program targeting breast cancer. The collaboration is notable because Astex previously attracted partnerships with Merck, AstraZeneca, Janssen, and Novartis—whose Kisqali blockbuster emerged from a mid-2000s collaboration. For operators and investors, the article signals Roche is reshaping its early R&D organization while simultaneously making targeted inlicensed bets to replenish its pipeline.

[Biotech](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech)

# Genentech lays off 103 alongside gRED rethink, pens $490M breast cancer collab

By
[James Waldron](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/person/james-waldron) Jul 6, 2026 7:30am

[Genentech](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/genentech) [layoffs](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/layoffs) [Roche](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/roche) [Licensing deals](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/licensing-deals)

[Share](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/genentech-sees-103-roles-impacted-gred-rethink-pens-490m-breast-cancer-collab#)

A month after Genentech confirmed the restructuring of its early development group, the Roche subsidiary has now disclosed that this change has formed part of a round of 103 layoffs.

The South San Francisco-headquartered business [already admitted](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/genentech-executes-another-round-layoffs-3-vps-axed) back in June that its vice presidents Vishva Dixit, M.D., Man-Wah Tan, Ph.D., and Todd McDevitt, Ph.D., were among the casualties of the latest round of layoffs. But Genentech declined to reveal at the time how many other positions would be affected.

Now, Genentech has told Fierce that “103 positions across several areas will be eliminated,” which include the changes at the Genentech Research and Early Development (gRED) group, “as well as in other parts” of the organization.

“It is important to emphasize that most of these are focused adjustments within Genentech impacting a small proportion of the organization and we continue to hire within Genentech, including gRED,” a spokesperson told Fierce over email. “Our hiring approach reflects the capabilities, expertise, and areas of science that are most critical to advancing our portfolio and delivering transformative medicines to patients now and in the future.”

The 103 layoffs are just the latest round of restructuring at Genentech, which last year issued [pink slips](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/genentech-cut-least-489-jobs-last-year-new-disclosure-reveals) to at least 489 employees, with many of the changes handed down by Roche in a global manufacturing reshuffle. Back in April of 2024, a 3% workforce reduction by Roche [affected](https://www.bizjournals.com/sanfrancisco/news/2024/04/10/genentech-roche-job-cuts-cancer.html) 436 Genentech employees.

Genentech confirmed the latest layoffs as it agreed to hand $25 million to Otsuka Pharmaceutical subsidiary Astex Pharmaceuticals as part of pact to discover new breast cancer drugs.

Astex’s fragment-based drug discovery platform has previously attracted Big Pharma partners [like Merck & Co](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/uks-astex-adds-merck-partnership-35m-upfront-tumour-suppressor-protein-candidates)., AstraZeneca and Janssen, as well as Novartis Institutes for BioMedical Research—with whom it collaborated on the now-blockbuster breast cancer drug Kisqali in the mid-2000s.

Today’s deal will see Cambridge, U.K.-based Astex in line for up to $490 million in milestone payments from Genentech, as well as tiered royalties on net sales of any resulting medicines.

The collaboration will involve Astex granting Genentech the exclusive license to compounds from Astex’s existing drug discovery program for breast cancer, with the two organizations working together to “accelerate the further optimization and development of lead compounds towards the identification of preclinical candidates,” according to a July 6 release.

Genentech will then take ownership of these programs for preclinical development and beyond.

“We are committed to pushing the boundaries of scientific innovation and turning breakthroughs into better outcomes for people with breast cancer,” Boris Zaïtra, Head of Roche Corporate Business Development, said in the release.

“Working alongside partners like Astex Pharmaceuticals in pursuit of precision therapies against cell-cycle regulators, we follow the science to focus our innovation on the areas of the highest unmet need,” Zaïtra added.

title: Kailera’s oral GLP-1 asset sees 11% weight loss, hits goals in Chinese phase 3 obesity and diabetes trials
summary: Kailera Therapeutics announced that its oral GLP-1 receptor agonist HRS-7535/KAI-7535, developed in China by partner Jiangsu Hengrui Pharmaceuticals, achieved 10.9% mean weight loss at Week 44 versus 2.5% for placebo in a phase 3 obesity trial; an ad hoc analysis showed 11.1% at Week 50. A separate phase 3 study in Type 2 diabetes patients demonstrated up to 1.68% HbA1c reduction, superior to the active comparator dapagliflozin (1.28%). Gastrointestinal adverse events were notable—nausea affected 70% of treated patients versus 16.2% on placebo, with vomiting at 68.6% versus 4.5%. The head-to-head figures sit in the same range as Eli Lilly’s Foundayo (11.1% at Week 72) and Novo Nordisk’s oral Wegovy (13.6% at Week 64), suggesting competitive parity on efficacy where tolerability may drive differentiation. Kailera raised $625 million in an April IPO and holds global rights outside Greater China; phase 2 topline data for its second oral candidate is expected next year, with Hengrui poised to file for regulatory approval in China.

[Biotech](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech)

# Kailera’s oral GLP-1 asset sees 11% weight loss, hits goals in Chinese phase 3 obesity and diabetes trials

By
[Nick Paul Taylor](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/person/nick-paul-taylor) Jul 7, 2026 8:44am

[Kailera Therapeutics](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/kailera-therapeutics) [Jiangsu Hengrui Pharmaceuticals](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/jiangsu-hengrui-medicine) [GLP-1](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/glp-1) [Obesity](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/obesity)

[Share](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/fierce-biotech-layoff-tracker-2026#)

![Yellow tape measure with a variety of weight loss medication on blue background](https://qtxasset.com/cdn-cgi/image/w=717,h=403,f=auto,fit=crop,g=0.5x0.5/https://qtxasset.com/quartz/qcloud5/media/image/GettyImages-1348209410.jpg?VersionId=ULXrxYF8OKCZEpCYyowVdLQrz2EO2vmh)The readouts expand the evidence on one of two oral weight-loss drugs in development at Kailera. (iStock/Getty Images Plus)

Phase 3 trials of Kailera Therapeutics’ oral GLP-1 prospect have [hit](https://www.globenewswire.com/news-release/2026/07/07/3323036/0/en/kailera-therapeutics-announces-positive-topline-data-from-two-hengrui-pharma-phase-3-clinical-trials-of-oral-small-molecule-glp-1-receptor-agonist-hrs-7535-kai-7535.html) their primary endpoints, positioning the biotech’s partner Hengrui Pharma to seek approval in obesity and diabetes in China.

Hengrui is developing the once-daily oral small molecule GLP-1 receptor agonist HRS-7535, which Kailera calls KAI-7535, in Greater China. The Chinese biotech shared data on Tuesday from two phase 3 trials, one in adults with obesity or overweight, and one in adults with Type 2 diabetes. Kailera, which has rights to the asset outside Greater China, began a phase 2 trial in people with obesity or overweight in April.

Hengrui’s obesity study linked HRS-7535 to mean weight loss of up to 10.9% at Week 44, compared to 2.5% for placebo. Weight loss climbed to 11.1% at Week 50 in an ad hoc analysis. The analyses excluded data collected after premature treatment discontinuations or the use of drugs with an obvious effect on weight. Including those data, weight loss on the high dose of HRS-7535 slipped to 9.8% at Week 44.

The weight loss results are in the same ballpark as data on existing oral GLP-1 drugs, namely Eli Lilly’s Foundayo and Novo Nordisk’s Wegovy pill. Lilly [linked](https://www.accessdata.fda.gov/drugsatfda_docs/label/2026/220934Orig1s000lbl.pdf) (PDF) Foundayo to weight loss of up to 11.1% at Week 72. Novo [reported](https://www.novo-pi.com/wegovy.pdf) (PDF) 13.6% weight loss at Week 64.

At a Jefferies event last month, Kailera CEO Ronald Renaud predicted that most oral drugs “are going to have weight loss in a very tight band, somewhere between 10% and 15%.” With multiple drugs offering similar weight loss, safety and tolerability is “where the differentiation happens,” Renaud said.

Seventy per cent of patients taking HRS-7535 in the obesity trial had nausea, making it the most common treatment-emergent adverse event. The nausea rate on placebo was 16.2%. Meanwhile, vomiting affected up to 68.6% of people on HRS-7535, compared to 4.5% of participants on placebo. The diarrhea figures for HRS-7535 and placebo were 36.9% and 15.3%, respectively.

For comparison, nausea, constipation, diarrhea and vomiting, the four most common adverse events in Lilly’s phase 3 program, affected 24% to 35% of participants. Novo [reported](https://pro.novonordisk.ae/multimedia-resources/articles-and-news/oral-semaglutide-25-mg-obesityweek-findings.html) nausea and vomiting rates of 46.6% and 30.9%, respectively, in a phase 3 trial of oral Wegovy.

Hengrui reported the obesity data alongside results from a phase 3 trial of HRS-7535 in people with Type 2 diabetes. Excluding data collected after premature treatment discontinuations or the use of other glucose-lowering medications, HRS-7535 achieved reductions in HbA1c—a measure of blood sugar—of up to 1.68%. HbA1c fell by 1.28% on dapagliflozin, an SGLT2 inhibitor that AstraZeneca sells as Farxiga.

The readouts expand the evidence on one of two oral weight-loss drugs in development at Kailera. The biotech is also advancing an oral peptide that targets GLP-1 and GIP. Kailera, which [raised](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/kailera-raises-head-turning-625m-ipo-fund-obesity-pipeline) an impressive $625 million in an IPO in April, could report topline phase 2 data on the oral candidates next year.

# Satellos’ ‘intriguing’ Duchenne data raise hopes ahead of key readout

By [Nick Paul Taylor](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/person/nick-paul-taylor)Jul 8, 2026 09:48am

[Clinical Data](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/clinical-data) [Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD)](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/duchenne-muscular-dystrophy-dmd)

[Share](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/fierce-biotech-layoff-tracker-2026#)

![Duchenne muscular dystrophy, DMD](https://qtxasset.com/cdn-cgi/image/w=717,h=403,f=auto,fit=crop,g=0.5x0.5/https://qtxasset.com/quartz/qcloud5/media/image/DUCHENNE.jpg?VersionId=gcxlkETQZuNUWdWA33imXxwajskNkdpU)One-year data on the four adult patients are also due in the fourth quarter. (Stock photo/Getty Images)

Satellos Bioscience has [shared](https://ir.satellos.com/news/news-details/2026/Satellos-Reports-Six-Month-Interim-TRAILHEAD-Data-Showing-Reduced-Muscle-Fat-Fraction-Increased-Effort-Stable-Strength-Lower-CK-and-Favorable-Safety-Profile-in-DMD-Adults-Treated-with-SAT-3247/default.aspx) evidence that its Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD) drug candidate is active in adults, leading analysts to predict the molecule “could re-imagine the ... therapeutic landscape” if the biotech replicates the data in children.

The adult data come from four people aged 21 to 28 years old who completed a phase 1a/b study of SAT-3247. The orally administered small molecule targets AAK1 to address problems with stem cell division caused by dystrophin deficiency, promoting muscle repair and regeneration. Satellos previously [shared](https://satellos.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/MDA-CL-101-Presentation-FINAL.pdf) (PDF) early evidence of improvements in the grip strength and lung capacity of adult patients taking SAT-3247.

Toronto-based Satellos used the International Congress on Neuromuscular Diseases to [share](https://satellos.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Farwell-ICNMD_Poster_FINAL.pdf) (PDF) six-month data on the adults. All four patients showed improvements across multiple measures of DMD, including decreased fat fraction (FF) in the elbow flexor. The DMD data impressed Guggenheim Securities analysts, who called the results intriguing and consistent.

“In our view, the consistency of effect across this diverse set of endpoints is suggestive of a true drug effect—an impressive feat given the advanced disease state of these patients,” the analysts said in a note to investors. “To the best of our knowledge, other DMD therapies have only been able to slow the rate of fat deposition but did not result in a reduction in FF.”

Satellos is most encouraged by the change in muscle FF as measured by MRI scan, Wildon Farwell, M.D., the biotech’s chief medical officer, said on a call with investors to discuss the data. The FF measure [is the primary endpoint](https://www.mdaconference.org/abstract-library/lb-trailhead-an-open-label-phase-2-study-evaluating-long-term-safety-and-efficacy-of-sat-3247-in-adults-with-duchenne-muscular-dystrophy-dmd/) of the Trailhead phase 2 trial that Satellos is running to assess SAT-3247 in adults. DMD patients are more likely to lose function when FF increases, Farwell said.

The adult data are “a good harbinger for the forthcoming Basecamp study” in boys aged 7 to 10 years, Guggenheim analysts said. Satellos expects to complete enrollment in Basecamp in the third quarter and post initial topline data from the study in the fourth quarter. One-year data on the four adult patients are also due in the fourth quarter.

# Scipher picks up Chemomab’s rheumatoid arthritis drug, Nasdaq listing in all-stock merger

By [James Waldron](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/person/james-waldron)Jul 8, 2026 09:26am

[mergers and acquisitions](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/mergers-and-acquisitions) [Rheumatoid Arthritis](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/rheumatoid-arthritis) [Autoimmune Diseases](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/autoimmune-diseases)

[Share](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/fierce-biotech-layoff-tracker-2026#)

![Deal](https://qtxasset.com/cdn-cgi/image/w=717,h=403,f=auto,fit=crop,g=0.5x0.5/https://qtxasset.com/quartz/qcloud5/media/image/Deal_0.jpg?VersionId=8Q0kurbUwkMSD9YDhexJpkipdHY7BXsn)Schipher CEO Reginald Seeto said that CCL24 had been identified by the Massachusetts company’s AI platform as “the highest-ranked clinical stage RA target for efficacy.” (MicroStockHub/iStock/Getty Images Plus)

Precision immunology company Scipher Medicine will absorb Chemomab Therapeutics after having identified the Israeli biotech’s rheumatoid arthritis (RA) drug as the ideal fit for its own expertise.

The merged entity will retain the Scipher brand and take advantage of Chemomab’s Nasdaq listing, although the ticker will switch to “SCIP.” The new company’s primary focus will be Chemomab’s RA asset nebokitug.

The plan is to take the anti-CCL24 antibody into a fresh phase 2 study that will also leverage Scipher’s multi-omic PrismRA blood test to help identify the most suitable patients. A topline readout from that study is penciled in for the first half of 2028, according to a [July 8 release](https://www.globenewswire.com/news-release/2026/07/08/3323986/0/en/chemomab-therapeutics-and-precision-medicine-pioneer-scipher-medicine-announce-merger-agreement-to-advance-nebokitug-in-an-ai-powered-phase-2-trial-in-rheumatoid-arthritis.html).

Chemomab has already taken nebokitug into a separate mid-stage trial, where the drug “demonstrated a favorable safety and tolerability profile and improvement in both inflammatory and fibrotic-related biomarkers,” the companies said in this morning’s release.

Schipher CEO Reginald Seeto—who will lead the merged business—said that CCL24 had been identified by the Massachusetts company’s AI precision medicine platform as “the highest-ranked clinical stage RA target for efficacy.”

“We believe this combination will enable us to advance nebokitug as the first precision medicine for the millions of RA patients whose disease is not well treated by current medications,” Seeto added in the release. “Notably, there have been no new novel mechanisms approved in RA by the FDA since 2012, and no new branded FDA approvals in RA since 2019.”

Meanwhile, Chemomab CEO Adi Mor, Ph.D.—who will join the board of the merged company—said he believed the merger “provides our shareholders a compelling potential opportunity to realize value through the clinical advancement of nebokitug in a large indication with substantial unmet need and well-established clinical endpoints as well as through Scipher’s revenue-generating precision medicine business and its strong biopharma partnerships.”

“Additionally, the opportunity remains to secure a potential partner for a phase 3 trial in primary sclerosing cholangitis, an indication with no FDA-approved therapies,” Mor pointed out.

Under the deal, which has been signed off by the boards of both companies, Chemomab’s shareholders will own 32% of the merged company and will also be in line for potential milestone payouts related to nebokitug’s progress. Scipher’s current equity holders will own 68% of the new company.

The combined company is expected to have a value of $150 million, ahead of an incoming $30 million from a private placement.

# Fierce Biotech Layoff Tracker 2026: Novartis loses 322 US staffers; BioCryst ends internal programs

By [Gabrielle Masson](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/person/gabrielle-masson-0) [, James Waldron](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/person/james-waldron) [, Will Maddox](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/person/will-maddox) [, Darren Incorvaia](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/person/darren-incorvaia)Jul 8, 2026 07:30am

[Big Pharma](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/big-pharma) [biopharma industry](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/biopharma-industry) [layoffs](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/layoffs) [Restructure](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/restructure)

[Share](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/fierce-biotech-layoff-tracker-2026#)

![x](https://qtxasset.com/cdn-cgi/image/w=717,h=403,f=auto,fit=crop,g=0.5x0.5/https://qtxasset.com/quartz/qcloud5/media/image/GettyImages-1682014762_0.jpg?VersionId=dkIDyc0B.4ZnasKOzZx1Wdhp7SxM2jV8)Could 2026 be the year that industry layoffs start to decline? (iStock/Getty Images Plus)

Welcome to Fierce Biotech’s fifth annual layoff tracker, a resource designed to keep pace with the sector’s steady stream of workforce reductions.

In 2025, industry layoffs continued to rise year over year, prompting the need for another edition of this article.

As always, please reach out to the Fierce Biotech editorial team with any news of a biopharma workforce reduction. We are committed to protecting the anonymity of all tips, but we do conduct further reviews to verify all information we receive.

_Editor’s note about the numbers:_ Layoff rounds that are part of a company’s larger, already-announced reorganization are not included in the monthly total count.

### July—1 company

**July 7 - Novartis.** The latest round of layoffs at the Swiss pharma's U.S. headquarters in East Hanover, New Jersey sees 322 pink slips sent out, mainly affecting the field sales, patient support and marketing organizations. [Story](https://www.fiercepharma.com/pharma/novartis-dismisses-322-more-its-us-headquarters-nj)

**July 6 - Genentech.** A month after confirming the restructuring of its early development group (see June entry below), the Roche subsidiary has now disclosed that this change has formed part of a round of 103 layoffs. [Story](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/genentech-sees-103-roles-impacted-gred-rethink-pens-490m-breast-cancer-collab)

### June—8 companies

**June 29 - BioCryst:** A month after ending the development of its diabetic macular edema treatment avoralstat, the company announced it was shuttering all internal discovery programs to focus on external innovation and partnerships. The change will result in the closure of BioCryst's Discovery Center of Excellence facility in Birmingham, Alabama by the end of this year, though the biopharma didn't share how many positions would be impacted. [Story](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/biocryst-ends-internal-discovery-programs-pivot-cost-saving-partnerships)

**June 26 - Biogen:** About a month after finalizing its Apellis buyout, Biogen is shrinking the R&D vision of the acquired biotech. The company is ending most of Apellis’ research programs and eliminating a “small number” of research roles, a spokesperson told Fierce. The changes will allow Biogen to focus on Apellis’ approved drugs Syfovre and Empaveli. [Story](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/biogen-ends-apellis-research-programs-cuts-small-number-roles-after-56b-buyout)

**June 24 - ADC Therapeutics:** In the wake of a recent safety scare in a confirmatory trial of its antibody-drug conjugate Zynlonta, the Swiss ADC specialist unveiled a reorganization scheme that includes layoffs for around 17% of the company's global workforce. [Story](https://www.fiercepharma.com/pharma/amid-safety-concerns-adc-therapeutics-plots-17-staff-cuts-push-resources-behind-lead-adc)

**June 23 - Sangamo Therapeutics:** The beleaguered genomic medicine biotech is selling off assets to Eli Lilly and Astellas Pharma while also saying goodbye to around 51 employees as part of a bankruptcy process. [Story](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/sangamo-puts-substantially-all-assets-sale-bankruptcy-lines-lilly-and-astellas-buyers)

**June 15 - enGene:** As enGene works toward a potential FDA filing for its bladder cancer gene therapy detalimogene, the company is trimming ranks to preserve cash. In a Monday release, the company detailed plans to cut half its workforce. It's also parting ways with several high-profile execs. The cuts are intended to allow the company to stay nimble until it can get its hands on 12-month data for the candidate and meet with the FDA regarding a Biologics License Application. EnGene anticipates submitting an approval filing for the gene therapy prospect this year. [Release](https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20260615436479/en/enGene-Reports-Second-Quarter-2026-Financial-Results-and-Provides-Business-Update)

**June 15 - Neumora Therapeutics:** Alongside reporting Monday that navacaprant failed a pair of phase 3 trials, Neumora said it's cutting around 35% of its staff. The reductions are aimed at saving around $10 million annually, with one-time reorganization expenses projected to hit $2 million. Neumora is now focusing on assets in Alzheimer's disease agitation, schizophrenia and obesity. [Story](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/neumora-lay-35-staff-after-navacaprant-goes-0-3-phase-3-depression-studies)

**June 11 - Genentech:** Vice presidents Vishva Dixit, M.D., Man-Wah Tan, Ph.D., and Todd McDevitt, Ph.D., were among the casualties in another round of layoffs at the South San Francisco subsidiary of Roche. The departures are part of a restructuring of the Genentech Research and Early Development (gRED) group. A spokesperson would not confirm how many positions are affected, but said “targeted adjustments” are being made to “ensure our capabilities and investments are aligned with our core therapeutic areas and portfolio priorities.” [Story](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/genentech-executes-another-round-layoffs-3-vps-axed)

**June 4 - Fulcrum Therapeutics**: After scrapping its only clinical-stage program following a hardline stance from the FDA, the sickle cell disease-focused biotech is drastically reducing its workforce by 85% as it begins searching for strategic alternatives. [Filing](https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1680581/000119312526257376/fulc-20260531.htm) and [Story](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/fulcrum-scraps-scd-program-considers-options-after-fda-takes-hard-line-safety-risks)

### May—4 companies

**May 15 - Novartis:** After a series of staff consolidation moves in recent weeks, the Swiss pharma is again trimming its ranks, albeit in what appears to be a more limited move centered on the drugmaker’s biomedical research arm. [Story](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/novartis-cuts-select-number-roles-biomedical-research-arm)

**May 13 - Valneva:** The French vaccine maker's “further restructuring plan \[is\] designed to streamline its global business operations.” It comes against the backdrop of “an emerging adverse trend in travel vaccine uptake across key markets,” and will include a reduction in global headcount of between 10% and 15%. [Story](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/valneva-lay-15-workforce-face-adverse-trend-travel-vaccine-uptake)

**May 13 - Takeda:** The Japanese pharma filled in details on its 2026 “transformation program,” which looks to centralize corporate functions, reduce management layers and target process simplification. The multiyear slim-down is expected to hit about 4,500 roles during the 2026 fiscal year. [Story](https://www.fiercepharma.com/pharma/takeda-slimming-down-new-era-plots-4500-layoffs-latest-restructuring-drive)

**May 13 - Gilead:** With the ink [now dry](https://investors.gilead.com/news/news-details/2026/Gilead-Sciences-Completes-Acquisition-of-Arcellx-Ahead-of-Potential-Commercial-Launch-of-Anito-cel/default.aspx) on the $7.8 billion buyout of CAR-T biotech Arcellx, Gilead will lay off 108 workers from Arcellx’s site at 800 Bridge Parkway in Redwood City, CA, and 84 employees at the 800 King Farm Boulevard site in Rockville, MD. [Story](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/after-arcellx-buyout-close-gilead-trims-108-jobs-car-t-biotechs-redwood-city-outpost)

**May 5 - BioNTech:** In the face of shrinking COVID-19 vaccine sales, German company BioNTech is slashing its manufacturing footprint, cutting 1,860 jobs while pulling out of its manufacturing sites in Singapore, the German cities of Idar-Oberstein and Marburg as well as at its CureVac subsidiary’s former headquarters in Tübingen. [Story](https://www.fiercepharma.com/manufacturing/biontech-tightens-its-manufacturing-belt-pulling-out-sites-germany-singapore-1860)

### April—4 companies

**April 30 - Autolus Therapeutics:** The CAR-T cell therapy outfit is shrinking its staff by 13% across the business as part of a plan to cut costs. The cuts will “enhance our margins, support scalable growth, and position Autolus for long-term value creation,” CEO Christian Itin, Ph.D., said. [Release](https://autolus.gcs-web.com/news-releases/news-release-details/autolus-therapeutics-announces-initiative-support-operational)

**April 28 - Passage Bio:** After some tough feedback from the FDA on the path forward for its lead gene therapy, Passage Bio has launched a strategic review and set out a plan to whittle down its headcount by 75%. [Story](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/passage-launches-strategic-review-and-75-layoffs-after-gene-therapy-path-blocked-fda)

**April 21 - Replimune:** The oncology biotech is shedding an additional 161 employees, or 60% of its workers, across two Massachusetts sites as the fallout continues from another FDA rejection of its lead melanoma candidate. [Story](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/replimune-sheds-60-staff-after-fda-rejection-vows-continue-operations)

**April 14 - Astellas Pharma:** The Japanese pharma is preparing to close the Seattle site of its stem cell therapy unit, with around 55 employees set to be impacted. [Story](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/astellas-close-stem-cell-units-seattle-site-layoffs-incoming)

**April 13 - Replimune:** After facing a "disappointing" second rejection for its melanoma prospect, the Massachusetts biotech is saying goodbye to 63 employees. [Story](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/fda-refuses-replimunes-melanoma-prospect-second-time)

### March—16 companies

**March 31 - Iterum Therapeutics:** The antibiotic specialist has started winding down its operations, citing limited cash amid the expensive commercialization of its only approved product, Orlynvah. [Story](https://www.fiercepharma.com/pharma/iterum-initiates-wind-down-after-failure-offload-antibiotic-sluggish-sales?_gl=1*1qc2m46*_gcl_au*NTM5MzYzOTMzLjE3NjcyODgzODY.*_ga*NTM4ODA3NjQ0LjE2NjQ5NTg5ODY.*_ga_KG49J84SR4*czE3NzUwMjkyMzMkbzE2MTgkZzEkdDE3NzUwMzY0MzkkajYwJGwwJGgw)

**March 31 - IO Biotech:** After its cancer vax was spurned by the FDA and following deep layoffs, IO Biotech is closing up shop for good, terminating all employees and executives. [Story](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/after-reeling-fda-refusal-io-biotech-surrenders-bankruptcy)

**March 31 - Lipella Pharmaceuticals:** The Pittsburgh-based company's ambition to develop the first treatment for an inflammatory mouth condition has reached the end of the road, with the biotech filing for bankruptcy. [Story](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/lipella-files-bankruptcy-ending-dream-developing-mouth-inflammation-drug)

**March 30 - Takeda:** Takeda plans to cut 634 jobs in the U.S. as part of a massive restructuring designed to save the company more than 200 billion Japanese yen ($1.26 billion) annually. The roles are assigned to the company’s U.S. headquarters in Cambridge, Massachusetts, and include 247 that are currently located in the Bay State, according to a WARN notice. Employee notifications have begun, but the changes will not take effect until July 2026, after new CEO Julie Kim officially takes the helm. [Story](https://www.fiercepharma.com/pharma/takeda-begins-us-layoffs-part-massive-13b-restructuring)

**March 17 - Gossamer Bio:** The biotech is cutting its workforce by 48% after the company’s phase 3 study of seralutinib in patients with pulmonary arterial hypertension missed its primary endpoint. Gossamer is evaluating strategic options and conserving cash with the 77-role reduction. [Source](https://ir.gossamerbio.com/node/9746/html#ic847212dae704ad3b44bc4591516367d_13)

**March 17 - Bicycle Therapeutics:** The biotech is letting more air out of its operations, with plans to deflate its employee count by about 30% and shelve its lead anti-cancer drug conjugate. The reprioritization is meant to halve yearly operating costs and will likely impact about 86 roles. [Story](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/hitting-regulatory-roadblock-bicycle-pumps-brakes-lead-program-and-deflates-headcount-30)

**March 12 - Inovio:** The Pennsylvania-based biotech said it had "eliminated roles that don't directly support" its primary goal of securing approval for its rare disease drug INO-3107. Annual reports from the company show the number of employees dropped to 112 people from a February 2025 figure of 134. [Story](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/inovio-trims-team-trouble-brews-fda-rare-disease-candidate)

**March 12 - Vistagen:** The Bay Area biotechis doubling down on its lead social anxiety candidate despite a phase 3 trial failure last December. The company is laying off about 20% of its staff to ensure it has the cash to finish another ongoing phase 3 trial of the anti-anxiety nasal spray candidate. [Story](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/vistagen-lays-20-staff-home-struggling-social-anxiety-candidate)

**March 11 - Amgen:** The California-based pharma is laying off 22 staffers from Horizon Therapeutics by early May, according to a Maryland WARN alert. Back in 2023, Amgen acquired the rare disease company in a $27.8 billion transaction.

"We are making organizational changes that will affect some roles at our Rockville research and development location," an Amgen spokesperson told Fierce. "These changes are part of a broader effort to align our operations with our research strategy to best serve our patients. We are working to support impacted employees through this transition." [Source](https://labor.maryland.gov/employment/warn.shtml)

**March 10 - Evotec:** The German company is escalating its already substantial reorganization, with plans to lay off 800 employees and shutter four sites in the next two years. [Story](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/evotec-reveals-sweeping-800-person-layoffs-more-site-closures-restructuring-rolls)

**March 10 - f5 Therapeutics:** The Molecular glue-focused biotech f5 Therapeutics has failed to stick around for the long haul, shuttering after six years. According to LinkedIn, the biotech employed up to 10 employees. [Story](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/molecular-glue-biotech-shutters-after-brutal-last-few-years-early-stage-companies)

**March 6 - Vertex Pharmaceuticals:** The pharma has laid off about 20 staffers in Massachusetts as part of a "functional reorganization," a spokesperson told Fierce Biotech, though overall Vertex's workforce in the state "continues to grow."

**March 5 - Alltrna:** After laying off 10% of its team last August, Flagship Pioneering-backed Alltrna is reducing its workforce by 19 people, or about 34%. The decision was made to “position Alltrna for success as we accelerate toward the clinic with our first engineered tRNA drug candidate,” a spokesperson told Fierce. [Story](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/alltrna-undergoes-third-layoff-round-entering-clinic-cutting-35-jobs)

**March 3 - EveryOne Medicines**: The individualized medicines biotech has closed its doors, according to Endpoints News, impacting an unknown number of workers. The shutdown comes of the heels of FDA details surrounding its [new approval pathway](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/fda-illuminates-new-approval-pathway-bespoke-gene-therapies) for bespoke gene therapies, guidance that fell below EveryOne's expectations, a person familiar with the matter told Endpoints. [Source](https://endpoints.news/everyone-medicines-shuts-down-ending-custom-cure-venture-that-banked-on-new-fda-pathway/)

**March 3 - Theravance Biopharma**: Following the phase 3 failure of its rare blood pressure disorder drug, the Dublin- and San Francisco-based biotech is cutting 50% of its employees, shutting down its R&D department and focusing commercial activity on its approved COPD treatment. The company said layoffs will be implemented over the following two quarters. [Story](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/theravance-halves-headcount-ends-rd-work-after-phase-3-rare-disease-fail)

**March 2 - BioAtla:** The San Diego-based company is laying off 70% of its workforce as the antibody biotech mulls whether to sell off its remaining assets. [Story](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/bioatla-lays-70-workforce-searches-buyers-antibody-pipeline)

### February—9 organizations

**Feb. 27 - Disc Medicine:** After an FDA rebuff, the rare disease biotechis laying off a fifth of its workforce, or about 30 people. The layoffs, which Disc expects to be completed in the second quarter, will primarily affect commercial roles and “certain supporting functions,” the company said. [Story](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/disc-lays-20-employees-it-steadies-ship-after-fda-rejection-rare-disease-drug)

**Feb. 27 - Merck & Co.:** Facing vaccine pressures in multiple markets, the pharma is pruning the ranks at one of its key U.S. production facilities. In a WARN alert, Merck revealed that it is laying off 147 staffers at its vaccine manufacturing plant in Durham, plus seven other employees who report to a different address in the city, which is part of North Carolina’s Research Triangle region. [Story](https://www.fiercepharma.com/manufacturing/merck-lay-nearly-150-staffers-research-triangle-area-vaccine-plant)

**Feb. 26 - Viatris:** After kicking off an enterprise-wide strategic review a year ago, Viatris plans to cut its global workforce of 32,000 by about 10% over the next three years. The company aims to save $600 million to $700 million annually once the plan is complete. [Story](https://www.fiercepharma.com/manufacturing/viatris-cut-10-workforce-3-year-overhaul-discloses-fire-india-plant)

**Feb. 17 - Concerto Biosciences**: The microbial-focused biotech is laying off an undisclosed number of staffers as it turns its focus to consumer products and applications, according to a statement shared by the company. New discovery and development work will fit into this consumer focus, prompting a workforce reduction to "better align" Concerto's resources with the shift.

**Feb. 12 - Ultragenyx**: Employees at the biopharma had already been warned at the end of December that the failure of a pair of phase 3 studies for its brittle bone disease candidate meant cost cutting was coming. It turns out this will involve laying off around 130 employees—equivalent to 10% of the company's total headcount. [Story](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/ultragenyx-lays-10-workforce-wake-phase-3-brittle-bone-trial-fails)

**Feb. 12 - Seres Therapeutics:** Just months after laying off a quarter of its workforce to focus resources on a phase 2 study of its bloodstream infection preventive treatment SER-155, Seres announced that it will shrink its headcount by a further 30% and halt investment in that trial. Instead, the company “will shift its operational focus to high-value earlier-stage pipeline programs.” [Story](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/seres-lay-30-employees-pause-lead-program-latest-strategic-shift)

**Feb. 5 - ARPA-H:** The federal research agency, which is designed to drive medical breakthroughs, has laid off an undisclosed number of staffers. "To ensure the agency remains focused on this, ARPA-H continuously evaluates its operations and has adjusted the term appointments of certain staff to better align with mission needs and research priorities," HHS spokesperson Andrew Nixon told Fierce Biotech. "These steps better position ARPA-H to deliver transformational health innovations."

**Feb. 2 - GSK:** The U.K. pharma is trimming its R&D team, a move expected to affect fewer than 50 workers in the U.K. and fewer than 70 staffers in the U.S. The ultimate number of cuts is still subject to change as the reorganization is finalized, but no more than 350 jobs in total will be affected, a company spokesperson told Fierce. [Story](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/gsk-plans-lay-350-rd-workers-across-us-uk)

**Feb. 2 - Thermo Fisher Scientific:** An ongoing layoff spree now includes a fresh set of cuts tied to a site that is shuttering in Franklin, Massachusetts. A company spokesperson told Fierce Pharma that the number of layoffs will be capped at 80 staffers. The site currently employs approximately 200 people. Following the cuts, the company expects the remaining roughly 120 employees to relocate to other facilities within the state. [Story](https://www.fiercepharma.com/manufacturing/thermo-fisher-trims-massachusetts-headcount-franklin-site-closure)

### January—11 companies

**Jan. 30 - Bitterroot Bio:** The California cardio-immunology company is restructuring as it goes from the clinic back to preclinical work, according to a post from departing CEO Pavan Cheruvu, M.D. The leader, alongside "many" other team members, will be impacted as Bitterroot turns "to focus on discovery biology and translational research," according to the post. Last year, the biotech had launched a [phase 2a trial](https://brbio.com/bitterroot-bio-announces-dosing-of-first-patient-in-matador-phase-2a-study-of-brb-002-an-immunomodulating-therapy-targeting-cd47-in-atherosclerotic-cardiovascular-disease/) for an immunomodulating protein drug designed to target CD47. It now appears to have abandoned the program, with Cheruvu writing that "in biotech, even with flawless execution, biology doesn't always go according to plan." [LinkedIn](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/pavan-cheruvu-117b5310a_after-nearly-4-years-as-ceo-at-bitterroot-activity-7422713135414976512-fP46/)

**Jan. 30 - IO Biotech:** The cancer vaccine specialist has brought in a financial advisor to aid in a strategic review while also implementing a "significant reduction" of the biotech's global workforce. At the same time, the biotech is letting go of Chief Medical Officer Qasim Ahmad, M.D., effective by the end of the day Feb. 15. [Release](https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1865494/000119312526031987/d13704d8k.htm)

**Jan. 20 - Takeda:** After a series of headcount reductions in recent years, Takeda has disclosed a new round of cuts expected to affect 243 field-based employees in 47 states and the District of Columbia tied to the looming patent cliff for antidepressant Trintellix (vortioxetine). The Japanese pharma is shifting resources to prepare for multiple potential medicine launches in the U.S., which could lead to 400 new commercial field roles. [Story](https://www.fiercepharma.com/pharma/takeda-cuts-243-us-workers-generic-competition-looms-trintellix)

**Jan. 16 - Vedanta Biosciences:** The Massachusetts biotech is “significantly” reducing its headcount to channel resources toward its lead live bacteria cocktail program, a spokesperson confirmed with Fierce. The move follows a 20% workforce reduction last summer after a separate treatment for Enterobacteriaceae failed to reduce disease severity in a phase 2 study. [Story](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/vedanta-significantly-reduces-headcount-fund-lead-bacteria-cocktail-program)

**Jan. 13 - Merck KGaA's EMD Serono:** Merck KGaA's North American division has laid off an undisclosed amount of employees from its research team at a site in Durham, North Carolina. The move follows last year’s acquisition of SpringWorks Therapeutics and is part of a decision to streamline research operations, a spokesperson confirmed with Fierce.

**Jan. 13 - Sonoma Biotherapeutics:** The regulatory T cell-focused biotech confirmed to Fierce that the company is reducing its workforce. While Sonoma wouldn't confirm how many employees will be affected, the biotech said roles across the organization and at "all levels and functions" would be impacted, including those based in South San Francisco, Seattle and remotely. The decision was made to "right-size the organization and align resources with SonomaBio’s highest priorities," such as a phase 1 study of its [lead rheumatoid arthritis asset](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/research/panacea-potential-how-nobel-winning-regulatory-t-cells-could-become-universal-drug).

**Jan. 12 - Lyra Therapeutics:** The Massachusetts-based biotech has “decided to suspend further development” of its chronic rhinosinusitis treatment. All 28 of Lyra’s remaining employees will be impacted by a “workforce reduction,” which is one of a number of “cost-saving actions to preserve capital.” [Story](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/lyra-ditches-sole-rhinosinusitis-asset-lays-all-employees)

**Jan. 8 - InflaRx:** The German inflammatory and immunology-focused biotech will reduce its headcount by about 30% and chart "substantial spending reductions" as it looks to channel resources toward its new lead asset, izicopan. It's unclear exactly how many employees will be affected; online sources put the company's total headcount at 74 about a year ago. [Story](https://www.fiercepharma.com/pharma/inflarx-prune-headcount-30-curb-covid-drug-spending-bid-advance-new-favorite-asset-izicopan)

**Jan. 8 - Rampart Bioscience:** After raising $125 million near the end of 2023, the genetic medicine biotech has appeared to wind down,Endpoints News reported after speaking with two former staffers. The U.S. biotech's website is no longer available. As of publication, Rampart's CEO Louis Breton has not responded to Fierce Biotech's request for comment. [Source](https://endpoints.news/secretive-rampart-bioscience-closes-after-pursuing-non-viral-gene-therapy/)

**Jan. 8 - Tessera Therapeutics:** The Flagship-founded company is planning to lay off 90 employees across a number of states starting March 8. The Massachusetts-based biotech isn’t expecting to close any of its facilities in connection with the layoffs. [Story](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/flagship-founded-tessera-plans-lay-90-employees-march)

**Jan. 2 - Nido Biosciences:** After its lead neurological disease candidate disappointed in a midstage study, the neurobiotech is closing its doors. “Just a handful of employees” will be impacted, a Nido spokesperson told Fierce. [Story](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/neuro-biotech-nido-closes-after-phase-2-study-fails-move-needle-rare-disease)

# AstraZeneca pens $2.1B Sino deal for challenger to Merck’s COPD drug Ohtuvayre

By [Nick Paul Taylor](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/person/nick-paul-taylor)Jul 8, 2026 05:00am

[AstraZeneca](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/astrazeneca) [chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD)](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/chronic-obstructive-pulmonary-disease-copd) [Licensing deals](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/licensing-deals) [Merck & Co.](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/merck-co)

[Share](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/fierce-biotech-layoff-tracker-2026#)

![AZ](https://qtxasset.com/cdn-cgi/image/w=717,h=403,f=auto,fit=crop,g=0.5x0.5/https://qtxasset.com/quartz/qcloud5/media/image/AstraZeneca_5.jpg?VersionId=Zn.jKHSMGqnpus0Oc7mi4cBIm2L.7mIM)The deal gives AstraZeneca exclusive global rights to certain future development programs. (CFOTO/Future Publishing/Getty Images)

AstraZeneca is paying Sino Biopharmaceutical $200 million upfront for ex-China rights to a late-phase challenger to Merck & Co.’s chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) drug Ohtuvayre.

The deal, which [includes](https://www1.hkexnews.hk/listedco/listconews/sehk/2026/0708/2026070800003.pdf) (PDF) up to $1.9 billion in milestones, covers a PDE3/4 inhibitor, TQC3721. Sino, working through its Chia Tai Tianqing Pharmaceutical subsidiary, has advanced a nebulized formulation of the drug candidate into phase 3 development in China. A dry powder inhaler formulation is in phase 2 and Sino has hailed TQC3721 as a potential best-in-class asset after [posting](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/41812987/) mid-stage data on the candidate.

Merck’s Ohtuvayre has set the benchmark in the PDE3/4 class. When the U.S. pharma [acquired](https://www.fiercepharma.com/pharma/merck-sweetens-its-portfolio-10b-acquisition-verona-and-potential-copd-blockbuster-ohtuvayre) Ohtuvayre last year in its $10 billion buyout of Verona Pharma, Merck CEO Robert Davis [said](https://onlinexperiences.com/scripts/Server.nxp?LASCmd=AI:4;F:QS!10100&ShowUUID=335C4D34-07B0-4915-B519-3B6265B5D62E&LangLocaleID=1033&Referrer=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.merck.com%2Finvestor-relations%2Fevents-and-presentations%2F) the asset has “multibillion-dollar peak commercial potential.” Ohtuvayre [generated](https://www.merck.com/news/merck-co-inc-rahway-n-j-usa-announces-first-quarter-2026-financial-results-highlights-significant-regulatory-approvals-and-clinical-milestones/) revenue of $131 million in the first quarter.

While Merck is racing to accelerate Ohtuvayre growth, AstraZeneca has spotted an opportunity to crash the PDE3/4 party. TQC3721 improved peak FEV1, a measure of lung function, by up to 147 mL at Week 4 of a phase 2 trial. For comparison, Verona [reported](https://www.veronapharma.com/news/verona-pharma-reports-positive-top-line-data-in-4-week-phase-2b-copd-study-with-nebulized-ensifentrine-on-top-of-tiotropium-therapy/) improvements of up to 124 mL in a phase 2 study.

Verona published its phase 2 data in 2020 and [won](https://www.fiercepharma.com/pharma/fda-nod-veronas-ohtuvayre-set-jack-copd-market) FDA approval for Ohtuvayre in 2024. AstraZeneca can draw on its COPD experience and expertise as it works to close the gap on Ohtuvayre. The U.K.-based pharma sells Symbicort and Breztri in indications including COPD, [generating](https://www.astrazeneca.com/content/dam/az/PDF/2026/eq1/Q1-2026-results-announcement.pdf) (PDF) sales of $1.2 billion from the drugs in the first quarter.

TQC3721 will join a pipeline of next-generation COPD drug candidates in development at AstraZeneca. In [March](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/astrazeneca-delivers-dream-result-phase-3-copd-hits-breathe-life-class-rocked-flops) and [April](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/astrazenecas-faith-il-33-inhibitor-continues-pay-latest-copd-phase-3-win), the company reported positive phase 3 data on its anti-IL-33 antibody tozorakimab, which it plans to send to regulators. AstraZeneca is also [running](http://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT07082738) a phase 2 trial of an IRAK4 inhibitor, AZD6793, in COPD.

The Sino deal also gives AstraZeneca exclusive global rights to certain future development programs.

# MeiraGTx scores $400M from Oberland Capital to help get 2 gene therapies to commercialization

By [Marissa Russo](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/person/marissa-russo)Jul 7, 2026 02:33pm

[MeiraGTx](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/meiragtx)

[Share](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/fierce-biotech-layoff-tracker-2026#)

![dollar sign with colored arrows in a maze](https://qtxasset.com/cdn-cgi/image/w=717,h=403,f=auto,fit=crop,g=0.5x0.5/https://qtxasset.com/quartz/qcloud5/media/image/GettyImages-92095106.jpg?VersionId=SsW2ge8JcfP7oOEOg4ovXTqhbucKS3N.)Oberland Capital is delivering an initial $125 million to MeiraGTx in exchange for low single-digit royalties on two gene therapy candidates. (iStock / Getty Images Plus)

MeiraGTx has secured an investment of up to $400 million from Oberland Capital to help get its late-stage gene therapies across the commercialization finish line.

Earlier this year, [MeiraGTx bought back](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/jj-pockets-25m-refund-meiragtx-struggling-eye-disease-gene-therapy) botaretigene sparoparvovec (bota-vec) from Johnson & Johnson, despite the therapy failing to improve vision-guided mobility in patients with X-linked retinitis pigmentosa (XLRP) in a [phase 3 trial](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/jj-gene-therapy-fails-improve-visual-navigation-late-stage-rare-eye-disease-trial). Despite the trial miss, the company touted “clinically meaningful benefit” seen in the data and said trial investigators are “enthusiastically supporting filing for regulatory approval of bota-vec” to support patient access.

Alongside this candidate, the company is developing [AAV2-hAQP1](https://meiragtx.com/programs-pipeline/xerostomia-dry-mouth/), a gene therapy for radiation-induced dry mouth. Both therapies have the potential for approval in the next 12 to 24 months, according to a July 7 [press release](https://investors.meiragtx.com/news-releases/news-release-details/meiragtx-announces-400-million-strategic-investment-oberland).

Under the new strategic investment, Oberland Capital is delivering an initial $125 million to MeiraGTx in exchange for low single-digit royalties on the candidates, plus a $10 million equity investment.

The gene therapy developer could score another $50 million upon receiving FDA approvals for each of its two candidates.

“We are very pleased to partner with Oberland Capital as we move towards potential commercialization of our late-stage programs for XLRP and radiation-induced xerostomia,” MeiraGTx president and CEO Alexandria Forbes, Ph.D., said in the release. “The size and terms of Oberland Capital’s investment demonstrate exceptional confidence in the strength of the data for these programs to date as well as the large commercial potential for both bota-vec and AAV2-hAQP1.

Additionally, $50 million will be available to MeiraGTx following a positive data readout from the phase 2 trial AQUAx2 in patients with dry mouth, with study completion estimated for the end of this year.

Beyond those funding tranches, the companies could add another $100 million to the pool by mutually agreeing to add products for development or undertaking a business development venture. Oberland Capital has the right to purchase an additional $15 million in equity in MeiraGTx, as well.

In addition to bota-vec and AAV2-hAQP1, MeiraGTx revealed promising [phase 2 data](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/meiragtx-gene-therapy-improves-motor-function-and-quality-life-phase-2-parkinsons-trial) in late 2024 on AAV-GAD, a gene therapy aimed at improving motor dysfunction in patients with Parkinson’s Disease. Following these results, the biotech launched a long-term follow-up study with this therapy, with study completion estimated for 2029.

“MeiraGTx is in the rare position of having three potentially approvable therapies within the next 12 to 24 months, two of which have significant commercial potential,” Michael Bloom, partner at Oberland Capital, said in the release. “Each of these would be first to market in areas of complete unmet need where there are a large number of patients waiting for these potential treatments.”

In November 2025, [Eli Lilly struck a deal](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/lilly-bags-eye-catching-retinal-disease-gene-therapy-meiragtx-475m-deal) with MeiraGTx to license AAV-AIPL1, a gene therapy designed for treatment of Leber congenital amaurosis type 4 (LCA4), a severe retinal disease present at birth, after data showing restoration of vision to 11 children who were born legally blind.

title: MeiraGTx scores $400M from Oberland Capital to help get 2 gene therapies to commercialization
summary: MeiraGTx has secured a $400 million strategic investment from Oberland Capital to fund commercialization of two late-stage gene therapies: botaretigene sparoparvovec (bota-vec) for X-linked retinitis pigmentosa and AAV2-hAQP1 for radiation-induced dry mouth. The deal consists of an initial $125 million in exchange for low single-digit royalties plus a $10 million equity stake, with another $50 million available per FDA approval for either candidate, $50 million contingent on a positive Phase 2 readout from the AQUAx2 dry mouth trial by year-end, and up to $100 million in additional funding via mutual product additions or business development. The company recently bought back bota-vec from Johnson & Johnson after its Phase 3 trial missed its primary endpoint of improving vision-guided mobility, though MeiraGTx cites 'clinically meaningful benefit' in the data and intends to file for regulatory approval. MeiraGTx also has AAV-GAD (Parkinson's) with promising Phase 2 data launching a long-term follow-up study, positioning three potentially approvable therapies within 12–24 months targeting areas of complete unmet need.

[Biotech](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech)

# MeiraGTx scores $400M from Oberland Capital to help get 2 gene therapies to commercialization

By
[Marissa Russo](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/person/marissa-russo) Jul 7, 2026 2:33pm

[MeiraGTx](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/meiragtx) [Biotech](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech)

[Share](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/meiragtx-scores-400m-oberland-capital-help-get-2-gene-therapies-commercialization#)

MeiraGTx has secured an investment of up to $400 million from Oberland Capital to help get its late-stage gene therapies across the commercialization finish line.

Earlier this year, [MeiraGTx bought back](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/jj-pockets-25m-refund-meiragtx-struggling-eye-disease-gene-therapy) botaretigene sparoparvovec (bota-vec) from Johnson & Johnson, despite the therapy failing to improve vision-guided mobility in patients with X-linked retinitis pigmentosa (XLRP) in a [phase 3 trial](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/jj-gene-therapy-fails-improve-visual-navigation-late-stage-rare-eye-disease-trial). Despite the trial miss, the company touted “clinically meaningful benefit” seen in the data and said trial investigators are “enthusiastically supporting filing for regulatory approval of bota-vec” to support patient access.

Alongside this candidate, the company is developing [AAV2-hAQP1](https://meiragtx.com/programs-pipeline/xerostomia-dry-mouth/), a gene therapy for radiation-induced dry mouth. Both therapies have the potential for approval in the next 12 to 24 months, according to a July 7 [press release](https://investors.meiragtx.com/news-releases/news-release-details/meiragtx-announces-400-million-strategic-investment-oberland).

Under the new strategic investment, Oberland Capital is delivering an initial $125 million to MeiraGTx in exchange for low single-digit royalties on the candidates, plus a $10 million equity investment.

The gene therapy developer could score another $50 million upon receiving FDA approvals for each of its two candidates.

“We are very pleased to partner with Oberland Capital as we move towards potential commercialization of our late-stage programs for XLRP and radiation-induced xerostomia,” MeiraGTx president and CEO Alexandria Forbes, Ph.D., said in the release. “The size and terms of Oberland Capital’s investment demonstrate exceptional confidence in the strength of the data for these programs to date as well as the large commercial potential for both bota-vec and AAV2-hAQP1.

Additionally, $50 million will be available to MeiraGTx following a positive data readout from the phase 2 trial AQUAx2 in patients with dry mouth, with study completion estimated for the end of this year.

Beyond those funding tranches, the companies could add another $100 million to the pool by mutually agreeing to add products for development or undertaking a business development venture. Oberland Capital has the right to purchase an additional $15 million in equity in MeiraGTx, as well.

In addition to bota-vec and AAV2-hAQP1, MeiraGTx revealed promising [phase 2 data](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/meiragtx-gene-therapy-improves-motor-function-and-quality-life-phase-2-parkinsons-trial) in late 2024 on AAV-GAD, a gene therapy aimed at improving motor dysfunction in patients with Parkinson’s Disease. Following these results, the biotech launched a long-term follow-up study with this therapy, with study completion estimated for 2029.

“MeiraGTx is in the rare position of having three potentially approvable therapies within the next 12 to 24 months, two of which have significant commercial potential,” Michael Bloom, partner at Oberland Capital, said in the release. “Each of these would be first to market in areas of complete unmet need where there are a large number of patients waiting for these potential treatments.”

In November 2025, [Eli Lilly struck a deal](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/lilly-bags-eye-catching-retinal-disease-gene-therapy-meiragtx-475m-deal) with MeiraGTx to license AAV-AIPL1, a gene therapy designed for treatment of Leber congenital amaurosis type 4 (LCA4), a severe retinal disease present at birth, after data showing restoration of vision to 11 children who were born legally blind.

title: Kalohexis submits confidential IPO filing to develop obesity rival to GLP-1s
summary: Kalohexis, a clinical-stage spinout of peptidic therapeutic company Endevica Bio spun out in March 2026, has confidentially submitted draft registration documents to the SEC for a proposed initial public offering to fund its pipeline of obesity and metabolic disease treatments. The lead asset is 710GO, an oral dual MC3R/MC4R (melanocortin receptor) agonist in Phase 1 development designed to reduce weight by changing the body's metabolic set-point rather than simply suppressing appetite like GLP-1 therapies such as Ozempic. A second candidate, mifomelatide, is an injectable dual MC3R/MC4R antagonist in Phase 2 for cancer cachexia (a wasting syndrome). CEO Russell Potterfield, who co-founded Endevica in 2015 and has been its lead since, will steer the publicly traded entity. Analysts view the IPO timing favorably given renewed investor appetite for biotech listings, including Kailera Therapeutics' recent $625 million offering.

[Biotech](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech)

# Endevica spinout Kalohexis submits confidential IPO filing to develop obesity challenger to GLP-1s

By
[Will Maddox](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/person/will-maddox) Jul 7, 2026 10:21am

[Obesity](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/obesity) [biotech IPO](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/biotech-ipo) [GLP-1](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/glp-1) [Kalohexis](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/kalohexis)

[Share](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/fierce-biotech-layoff-tracker-2026#)

![Measuring tape on top of cash](https://qtxasset.com/cdn-cgi/image/w=717,h=403,f=auto,fit=crop,g=0.5x0.5/https://qtxasset.com/quartz/qcloud5/media/image/GettyImages-1426421880.jpg?VersionId=BcMFM8bcqIgEDhe6CcK3Qgj.GQ.qkl0i)Kalohexis spun out of Endevica Bio in March to focus on metabolic disease by leveraging the body’s melanocortin system. (iStock / Getty Images Plus)

Clinical-stage biotech Kalohexis is preparing for a public listing to support its obesity and cancer cachexia treatments.

Though the company has not determined how many shares it plans to offer or the price, Kalohexis revealed in a July 7 [release](https://www.kalohexis.com/kalohexis-announces-confidential-submission-of-draft-registration-statement-for-proposed-initial-public-offering/) that it has confidentially submitted the relevant IPO documents to the Securities and Exchange Commission.

Kalohexis [spun out of](https://www.endevicabio.com/endevica-announces-spinout-of-kalohexis-to-advance-first-and-best-in-class-therapeutics-harnessing-the-melanocortin-system-to-treat-obesity-and-metabolic-diseases/) the privately owned peptide therapeutic company Endevica Bio in March to focus on treatments that leverage the body’s melanocortin system, which regulates metabolic homeostasis. Kalohexis has developed its own peptide candidates that target central melanocortin-3 and -4 receptors (MC3R/MC4R) to treat a range of metabolic disorders.

The biotech's lead assets are 710GO, an oral dual MC3R/MC4R agonist in phase 1 development for weight loss, and mifomelatide, an injectable dual MC3R/MC4R antagonist in phase 2 for the treatment of a wasting syndrome called cachexia in patients with advanced cancers. The company also has several preclinical discovery programs.

Back when the biotech spun out of Endevica, Kalohexis Chief Medical and Scientific Officer Daniel Marks, M.D., Ph.D., said the company's approach has shown advantages over GLP-1s in the obesity space.

“710GO has the potential to be a healthier, more durable obesity treatment than current standard of care because rather than just suppressing appetite, it changes the body's metabolic set-point,” Marks said at the time.

Marks also pointed to preclinical data that he claimed showed 710GO “did not cause the side effects common with GLP-1 receptor agonists, such as loss of lean body mass, gastrointestinal distress and rapid weight regain following treatment cessation.”

Kalohexis is led by Russell Potterfield, who has served as Endevica's CEO since 2020 after helping found the company in 2015.

Kalohexis' leadership will likely take heart from the renewed investor interest in biotech IPOs this year, which has included the [$625 million offering](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/kailera-raises-head-turning-625m-ipo-fund-obesity-pipeline) by obesity-focused Kailera Therapeutics.

# Satellos’ ‘intriguing’ Duchenne data raise hopes ahead of key readout

By [Nick Paul Taylor](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/person/nick-paul-taylor)Jul 8, 2026 09:48am

[Clinical Data](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/clinical-data) [Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD)](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/duchenne-muscular-dystrophy-dmd)

[Share](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/fierce-biotech-layoff-tracker-2026#)

![Duchenne muscular dystrophy, DMD](https://qtxasset.com/cdn-cgi/image/w=717,h=403,f=auto,fit=crop,g=0.5x0.5/https://qtxasset.com/quartz/qcloud5/media/image/DUCHENNE.jpg?VersionId=gcxlkETQZuNUWdWA33imXxwajskNkdpU)One-year data on the four adult patients are also due in the fourth quarter. (Stock photo/Getty Images)

Satellos Bioscience has [shared](https://ir.satellos.com/news/news-details/2026/Satellos-Reports-Six-Month-Interim-TRAILHEAD-Data-Showing-Reduced-Muscle-Fat-Fraction-Increased-Effort-Stable-Strength-Lower-CK-and-Favorable-Safety-Profile-in-DMD-Adults-Treated-with-SAT-3247/default.aspx) evidence that its Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD) drug candidate is active in adults, leading analysts to predict the molecule “could re-imagine the ... therapeutic landscape” if the biotech replicates the data in children.

The adult data come from four people aged 21 to 28 years old who completed a phase 1a/b study of SAT-3247. The orally administered small molecule targets AAK1 to address problems with stem cell division caused by dystrophin deficiency, promoting muscle repair and regeneration. Satellos previously [shared](https://satellos.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/MDA-CL-101-Presentation-FINAL.pdf) (PDF) early evidence of improvements in the grip strength and lung capacity of adult patients taking SAT-3247.

Toronto-based Satellos used the International Congress on Neuromuscular Diseases to [share](https://satellos.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/07/Farwell-ICNMD_Poster_FINAL.pdf) (PDF) six-month data on the adults. All four patients showed improvements across multiple measures of DMD, including decreased fat fraction (FF) in the elbow flexor. The DMD data impressed Guggenheim Securities analysts, who called the results intriguing and consistent.

“In our view, the consistency of effect across this diverse set of endpoints is suggestive of a true drug effect—an impressive feat given the advanced disease state of these patients,” the analysts said in a note to investors. “To the best of our knowledge, other DMD therapies have only been able to slow the rate of fat deposition but did not result in a reduction in FF.”

Satellos is most encouraged by the change in muscle FF as measured by MRI scan, Wildon Farwell, M.D., the biotech’s chief medical officer, said on a call with investors to discuss the data. The FF measure [is the primary endpoint](https://www.mdaconference.org/abstract-library/lb-trailhead-an-open-label-phase-2-study-evaluating-long-term-safety-and-efficacy-of-sat-3247-in-adults-with-duchenne-muscular-dystrophy-dmd/) of the Trailhead phase 2 trial that Satellos is running to assess SAT-3247 in adults. DMD patients are more likely to lose function when FF increases, Farwell said.

The adult data are “a good harbinger for the forthcoming Basecamp study” in boys aged 7 to 10 years, Guggenheim analysts said. Satellos expects to complete enrollment in Basecamp in the third quarter and post initial topline data from the study in the fourth quarter. One-year data on the four adult patients are also due in the fourth quarter.

# Scipher picks up Chemomab’s rheumatoid arthritis drug, Nasdaq listing in all-stock merger

By [James Waldron](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/person/james-waldron)Jul 8, 2026 09:26am

[mergers and acquisitions](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/mergers-and-acquisitions) [Rheumatoid Arthritis](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/rheumatoid-arthritis) [Autoimmune Diseases](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/keyword/autoimmune-diseases)

[Share](https://www.fiercebiotech.com/biotech/fierce-biotech-layoff-tracker-2026#)

![Deal](https://qtxasset.com/cdn-cgi/image/w=717,h=403,f=auto,fit=crop,g=0.5x0.5/https://qtxasset.com/quartz/qcloud5/media/image/Deal_0.jpg?VersionId=8Q0kurbUwkMSD9YDhexJpkipdHY7BXsn)Schipher CEO Reginald Seeto said that CCL24 had been identified by the Massachusetts company’s AI platform as “the highest-ranked clinical stage RA target for efficacy.” (MicroStockHub/iStock/Getty Images Plus)

Precision immunology company Scipher Medicine will absorb Chemomab Therapeutics after having identified the Israeli biotech’s rheumatoid arthritis (RA) drug as the ideal fit for its own expertise.

The merged entity will retain the Scipher brand and take advantage of Chemomab’s Nasdaq listing, although the ticker will switch to “SCIP.” The new company’s primary focus will be Chemomab’s RA asset nebokitug.

The plan is to take the anti-CCL24 antibody into a fresh phase 2 study that will also leverage Scipher’s multi-omic PrismRA blood test to help identify the most suitable patients. A topline readout from that study is penciled in for the first half of 2028, according to a [July 8 release](https://www.globenewswire.com/news-release/2026/07/08/3323986/0/en/chemomab-therapeutics-and-precision-medicine-pioneer-scipher-medicine-announce-merger-agreement-to-advance-nebokitug-in-an-ai-powered-phase-2-trial-in-rheumatoid-arthritis.html).

Chemomab has already taken nebokitug into a separate mid-stage trial, where the drug “demonstrated a favorable safety and tolerability profile and improvement in both inflammatory and fibrotic-related biomarkers,” the companies said in this morning’s release.

Schipher CEO Reginald Seeto—who will lead the merged business—said that CCL24 had been identified by the Massachusetts company’s AI precision medicine platform as “the highest-ranked clinical stage RA target for efficacy.”

“We believe this combination will enable us to advance nebokitug as the first precision medicine for the millions of RA patients whose disease is not well treated by current medications,” Seeto added in the release. “Notably, there have been no new novel mechanisms approved in RA by the FDA since 2012, and no new branded FDA approvals in RA since 2019.”

Meanwhile, Chemomab CEO Adi Mor, Ph.D.—who will join the board of the merged company—said he believed the merger “provides our shareholders a compelling potential opportunity to realize value through the clinical advancement of nebokitug in a large indication with substantial unmet need and well-established clinical endpoints as well as through Scipher’s revenue-generating precision medicine business and its strong biopharma partnerships.”

“Additionally, the opportunity remains to secure a potential partner for a phase 3 trial in primary sclerosing cholangitis, an indication with no FDA-approved therapies,” Mor pointed out.

Under the deal, which has been signed off by the boards of both companies, Chemomab’s shareholders will own 32% of the merged company and will also be in line for potential milestone payouts related to nebokitug’s progress. Scipher’s current equity holders will own 68% of the new company.

The combined company is expected to have a value of $150 million, ahead of an incoming $30 million from a private placement.
